topics.name: AI & ML
topics.intro: Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 — a public, Mythos-class model that tops nearly every capability benchmark — dominated the week, even as a brutal new UC Berkeley test showed frontier models still fail three-quarters of real professional work.

items.headline: Anthropic releases Claude Fable 5, its most powerful public model, topping SWE-bench Pro at 80.3% vs GPT-5.5's 58.6%
items.brief: Anthropic made Fable 5 — a version of its previously restricted Mythos-class system — generally available via the Claude API and Enterprise plans, calling it the most capable model it has ever shipped to the public. It scores 80.3% on SWE-bench Pro (against GPT-5.5's 58.6%), is the first model to break 90% on Cognition's long-running analytics eval, and matched GPT-5.5 on frontier physics research in 36 hours using a third of the reasoning tokens. New safeguards auto-redirect high-risk cyber, bio and chemistry prompts to weaker models, and Fable 5 is free on Pro, Max, Team and seat-based Enterprise plans through June 22.
items.whyItMatters: It puts near-frontier 'Mythos' capability in the hands of ordinary paying users for the first time — and resets the coding and knowledge-work bar rivals must clear.

items.headline: Google's DiffusionGemma generates text 4x faster by denoising 256 tokens at once instead of one at a time
items.brief: Google released DiffusionGemma, an open-weight 26B mixture-of-experts model (3.8B active params) under Apache 2.0 that abandons left-to-right token generation for diffusion: it starts from a 'canvas' of placeholder tokens and refines 256 in parallel per pass, self-correcting as it goes. It hits 1,000+ tokens/second on a single NVIDIA H100 and 700+ on a consumer RTX 5090, fitting in 18GB of VRAM when quantized, with weights on Hugging Face and support for vLLM, MLX, JAX and NVIDIA NeMo. The trade-off: it sacrifices some output quality versus standard Gemma 4 in exchange for speed.
items.whyItMatters: It pushes diffusion text generation to production scale on consumer hardware, pointing toward far cheaper, lower-latency local AI.

items.headline: UC Berkeley's new Agents' Last Exam shows top AI fails ~76% of real jobs; GPT-5.5 edges Fable 5, 24.0% to 22.0%
items.brief: UC Berkeley's Center for Responsible, Decentralized Intelligence launched the Agents' Last Exam (ALE), a 1,490-task benchmark built with 300+ domain experts across 55 professional sub-domains — from 3D modeling in Siemens NX to neuroimaging in FSLeyes and VFX compositing in After Effects. GPT-5.5, running through OpenAI's Codex agent framework, scored a 24.0% pass rate to claim the top spot, narrowly beating Claude Fable 5 at 22.0%. The benchmark is scaling toward 5,000 tasks.
items.whyItMatters: It's a reality check on agent hype: the world's best models still can't finish three-quarters of economically valuable, long-horizon professional work.

items.headline: Claude Fable 5 spins up playable video games from a single prompt in Ethan Mollick's tests
items.brief: Wharton professor Ethan Mollick demonstrated Fable 5 generating complete, playable games through Claude Code from one text prompt each: a working Snake clone, an underground lantern-lighting explorer called Strata that looks like 'a degraded version of Myst,' and Duino, a melancholy poetry game built around Rilke's verse. He also had it build an isochronic travel-time map, arguing that software projects that once needed whole teams are now 'spun up from a single prompt.'
items.whyItMatters: It's a concrete look at how far one-shot 'vibe coding' has come — and how much production software a single non-engineer can now generate.

items.headline: Google's Gemini 3.5 Live Translate does continuous speech-to-speech across 70+ languages, preserving the speaker's voice
items.brief: Google unveiled Gemini 3.5 Live Translate, an audio model that delivers near real-time speech-to-speech translation in 70+ languages and generates translated speech continuously rather than waiting for the speaker to finish — while preserving intonation, pacing and pitch. Ride-hailing firm Grab is testing it for driver-rider communication at pickups, and Google Meet will use it to support 2,000+ language combinations within a single meeting.
items.whyItMatters: Continuous, voice-preserving translation moves machine interpreting closer to a natural conversation, not a stilted turn-by-turn exchange.

topics.name: Startups & Tech
topics.intro: Coinbase opened crypto trading and autonomous payments to AI agents, headlining a week of product moves as Bluesky and Meta retooled around communities and creators.

items.headline: Coinbase launches 'Coinbase for Agents,' letting ChatGPT and Claude trade crypto and auto-pay for research via MCP and x402
items.brief: Coinbase debuted Coinbase for Agents on June 11, a tool that connects AI assistants like ChatGPT and Claude to a user's account through an MCP server (plus a CLI for terminal agents) to trade crypto spot and derivatives, rebalance portfolios, and follow an investment thesis, with a sandbox mode for testing. It also wires in x402, Coinbase's open machine-to-machine payment standard built with AWS, Anthropic, Circle and Near, so agents can pay for premium research, data APIs and on-demand compute without a login or subscription. 'We're aiming to build a fundamentally different product for a future where most of the internet is accessed through agents,' said Lincoln Murr, Coinbase's Head of AI Product, with equities and prediction markets planned next.
items.whyItMatters: It pushes 'agentic commerce' from demo to live infrastructure, letting autonomous agents move money and buy services on their own — and follows Robinhood's own agent-trading push.

items.headline: Bluesky launches 50-person group chats and pivots to 'communities' as growth stalls at 44.8M users
items.brief: Bluesky rolled out group chats supporting up to 50 members in app version v1.124 on June 11, with creators controlling access via invite links and media sharing disabled for moderation reasons. The feature is the first installment of a strategic shift toward communities — Bluesky plans public, invite-only and private communities, each with its own handle and URL — rather than positioning itself purely as a broadcast network rivaling X. The move comes as registered users have plateaued at 44.8 million, against X's roughly 600 million monthly actives.
items.whyItMatters: Bluesky is betting that tighter community tools, not raw reach, will revive engagement and differentiate it from X.

items.headline: Meta adds an AI assistant and a desktop version to its CapCut rival Edits to keep creators
items.brief: At an invite-only creator event in Los Angeles, Meta previewed a desktop version of its mobile-only video-editing app Edits plus an AI assistant that taps a creator's Instagram data — views and video-retention insights — to suggest video ideas and trending audio. Meta also shipped new tools the same day, including a 'Beta' tab for experimental features and expanded audience insights, while the AI assistant remains in testing with attendees and the desktop app is 'coming soon.' Edits launched last year as a direct competitor to ByteDance's CapCut.
items.whyItMatters: Meta is racing to lock in short-video creators with data-driven editing tools as CapCut's U.S. future stays uncertain.

topics.name: Markets & Finance
topics.intro: SpaceX's record $75 billion Nasdaq debut — the largest IPO in history — headlined a finance-heavy weekend as Elon Musk's rocket maker surged 19% and AI money kept flowing toward Jeff Bezos's Prometheus and a G7 gathering of AI chiefs.

items.headline: SpaceX raises $75 billion in largest IPO ever, closes up 19% at a ~$1.77 trillion valuation
items.brief: Elon Musk's SpaceX priced its IPO at $135 a share and sold 555.6 million shares to raise about $75 billion on June 12, then opened on the Nasdaq at $150 under ticker SPCX and closed up 19% near $161. The haul is nearly triple the previous record — Saudi Aramco's $29 billion 2019 listing — and the roughly $1.77 trillion valuation makes SpaceX one of the most valuable U.S. companies, larger than Tesla on day one.
items.whyItMatters: The blockbuster debut reopens the mega-IPO market and hands Musk a fresh, publicly traded currency for his satellite, rocket and AI ambitions.

items.headline: Oil drops over 3% as Trump officials see 80% odds of a U.S.-Iran deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz
items.brief: U.S. WTI crude fell 3.2% to settle at $84.88 a barrel and Brent lost 3.4% to $87.33 on June 12 as hopes grew for an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and lift the U.S. naval blockade. A senior Trump administration official put the chance of a signed deal in the coming days at 80%, while Iran's Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi said a memorandum of understanding has 'never been closer.' Crude remains up more than 20% since the conflict began.
items.whyItMatters: A Hormuz reopening would ease the energy-driven inflation spike that has kept the Fed on hold, but a collapse in talks could send oil back above $100.

items.headline: Jeff Bezos's AI startup Prometheus raises $12 billion at a $41 billion valuation
items.brief: Prometheus, the AI company Jeff Bezos co-leads as CEO, raised $12 billion in a funding round that values it at roughly $41 billion. Backers include JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock and Bezos himself, in one of the largest private AI raises of the year.
items.whyItMatters: It cements Bezos as a direct AI player alongside OpenAI and Anthropic and shows mega-rounds for frontier AI are still drawing Wall Street's biggest names.

items.headline: Altman, Amodei and Hassabis to attend the G7 summit in France on June 15-17
items.brief: OpenAI's Sam Altman, Anthropic's Dario Amodei and Google DeepMind's Demis Hassabis will attend the G7 leaders' summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, the first such gathering with all three frontier-AI chiefs present. President Emmanuel Macron personally invited Altman, and the summit is expected to produce voluntary pledges rather than binding rules on advanced-AI risks including cyber and biological threats.
items.whyItMatters: The meeting puts AI's most powerful executives in the same room as G7 leaders, shaping the light-touch regulatory regime their trillion-dollar valuations depend on.

items.headline: U.S. stocks close higher Friday as SpaceX pop and Iran optimism lift the S&P 500 to 7,431
items.brief: The S&P 500 rose 0.5% to 7,431.46, the Nasdaq Composite added 0.31% to 25,888.84 and the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 353.51 points, or 0.7%, to 51,202.26 on June 12. SpaceX's strong market debut and growing hopes for a U.S.-Iran peace deal drove the gains, with bank stocks among the leaders.
items.whyItMatters: Equities are pushing toward records even as inflation runs hot, setting up a tense backdrop for next week's Fed decision.

items.headline: Fed expected to hold rates at 3.5-3.75% on June 17 as CPI hits a three-year high near 4.2%
items.brief: Markets price a roughly 99% chance the Federal Reserve leaves its target range at 3.5-3.75% at the June 16-17 meeting, the first under new Chair Kevin Warsh. Consumer inflation has accelerated to a three-year high around 4.2%, with energy prices — largely oil — driving more than 60% of the latest monthly increase, pushing policymakers away from any easing bias.
items.whyItMatters: With inflation reaccelerating and the Fed signaling possible hikes later in 2026, hopes for rate cuts are fading even as oil prices threaten to spike again.

topics.name: Boxing
topics.intro: Boxing's biggest week ahead builds toward Xander Zayas vs. Jaron 'Boots' Ennis for the unified junior middleweight titles in Brooklyn on June 27, while Jesse 'Bam' Rodriguez just announced himself as a three-division champion.

items.headline: Jesse 'Bam' Rodriguez stops Antonio Vargas in 6 to win WBA bantamweight title, becomes three-division champ
items.brief: Rodriguez (24-0, 17 KOs) knocked out Antonio Vargas (19-2-1, 11 KOs) at 1:15 of the sixth round on June 13 at Desert Diamond Arena in Glendale, Arizona, on DAZN, to capture the WBA bantamweight belt. The win makes the 25-year-old a champion in a third weight class after titles at 108 and 115.
items.whyItMatters: Bam cements himself as one of the sport's pound-for-pound elite and opens up unification fights up at 118.

items.headline: Xander Zayas defends unified WBO/WBA junior middleweight titles vs. Jaron 'Boots' Ennis, June 27 in Brooklyn
items.brief: Unbeaten Xander Zayas (23-0, 13 KOs) puts his unified 154-pound titles on the line against former welterweight king Jaron 'Boots' Ennis (35-0, 31 KOs) on June 27 at Barclays Center, live on DAZN PPV. Zayas unified in January via split decision over Abass Baraou; Ennis moves up after one fight at the weight.
items.whyItMatters: Two undefeated fighters in their primes in the most significant 154-pound fight in years.

items.headline: Tommy Fury edges Eddie Hall by majority decision in Misfits exhibition, June 13 in Manchester
items.brief: Tommy Fury beat former World's Strongest Man Eddie Hall by majority decision (58-56, 59-56, 57-57) over six two-minute rounds at the AO Arena in Manchester, in the Misfits Boxing main event. The bout was an exhibition and does not go on Fury's pro record; chaos erupted in the ring afterward.
items.whyItMatters: Keeps Fury in the lucrative crossover spotlight while the result carries no competitive weight.

items.headline: Ryan Garner vs. Michael Magnesi for WBC interim super featherweight title, this Saturday June 20 at St Mary's
items.brief: Southampton's Ryan Garner (19-0, 10 KOs) faces Italy's Michael Magnesi for the WBC interim super featherweight title on June 20 on the pitch at St Mary's Stadium, home of Southampton FC, live on DAZN. It is a hometown 'dream' headliner for the unbeaten Garner.
items.whyItMatters: A win moves Garner a step from the full WBC 130-pound world title.

items.headline: Abdullah Mason defends WBO lightweight title vs. Joe Cordina, July 4 in Cleveland
items.brief: Boxing's youngest world champion, Abdullah Mason (22), makes his first WBO lightweight title defense against Welsh former world champion Joe Cordina (19-1, 9 KOs) on July 4 at Cleveland State University's Wolstein Center, airing on TNT and DAZN. Mason won the belt in November 2025 by beating Sam Noakes.
items.whyItMatters: A hometown coming-out party for one of the sport's brightest young stars against a seasoned ex-champ.

items.headline: Errol Spence Jr. returns from three years out vs. Tim Tszyu at junior middleweight, July 25 in Australia
items.brief: Former unified welterweight champion Errol Spence Jr. (28-1, 22 KOs) fights for the first time since his 2023 loss to Terence Crawford, meeting former 154-pound titlist Tim Tszyu (27-3, 18 KOs) on July 25 in Australia. The PBC junior middleweight bout streams on Prime Video via PPV.
items.whyItMatters: Spence's comeback against a name opponent will show whether he remains a force at 154.

items.headline: Anthony Joshua returns vs. Kristian Prenga, July 25 in Riyadh
items.brief: Two-time former heavyweight champion Anthony Joshua is set to return against Kristian Prenga on July 25 in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, on DAZN, in a non-title tune-up. The bout is positioned to set up an anticipated showdown with Tyson Fury.
items.whyItMatters: A get-back-in-rhythm fight aimed squarely at finally landing the all-British Fury megafight.

items.headline: Edwin De Los Santos vs. Jose Valenzuela at lightweight, June 28 in Las Vegas
items.brief: Lightweight punchers Edwin De Los Santos and Jose Valenzuela meet on June 28 in Las Vegas, streaming on Paramount+, in a non-title crossroads bout in a deep 135-pound division.
items.whyItMatters: The winner stays in the mix among a crowded lightweight title picture.

topics.name: MMA
topics.intro: MMA's biggest spectacle of the year lands tonight as Ilia Topuria defends the lightweight title against Justin Gaethje on the South Lawn of the White House.

items.headline: Ilia Topuria defends lightweight title vs. Justin Gaethje at the White House tonight (June 14)
items.brief: UFC Freedom 250 stages an unprecedented card on the White House South Lawn in Washington, D.C. on Sunday, June 14, with undefeated lightweight champion Ilia Topuria (17-0) facing interim champ Justin Gaethje (27-5) in the main event. In the co-main, Alex Pereira (13-3) chases a title in a third weight class against Ciryl Gane (13-2) for the interim heavyweight crown; the stacked card also features Sean O'Malley vs. Aiemann Zahabi, Diego Lopes vs. Steve Garcia and Michael Chandler vs. Mauricio Ruffy.
items.whyItMatters: A first-of-its-kind UFC event at the White House with two championships on the line and Pereira bidding for three-division history.

items.headline: Gabriel Bonfim upsets former champ Belal Muhammad by decision in June 6 welterweight headliner
items.brief: At UFC Fight Night: Muhammad vs. Bonfim on June 6 at the UFC Apex in Las Vegas, rising welterweight Gabriel Bonfim handed former welterweight champion Belal Muhammad a unanimous-decision loss in the main event. The win vaults Bonfim into the upper tier of a deep 170-pound division while raising questions about Muhammad's path back toward contention.
items.whyItMatters: A statement victory that reshapes the welterweight title picture and stalls a former champion's comeback.

items.headline: Manel Kape vs. Kyoji Horiguchi flyweight rematch headlines June 20 Fight Night in Las Vegas
items.brief: UFC Fight Night: Kape vs. Horiguchi runs June 20 at the UFC Apex in Enterprise, Nevada, topped by a flyweight rematch between Manel Kape and former title challenger Kyoji Horiguchi. The card also includes Andre Fili vs. Vinicius Oliveira at featherweight and Ion Cutelaba vs. Navajo Stirling at light heavyweight, streaming on Paramount+.
items.whyItMatters: A win keeps either flyweight in the mix for a 125-pound title shot.

items.headline: TSMC says ASML's $400M High-NA EUV is 'too expensive,' delays adoption until at least 2029
items.brief: Speaking at TSMC's North America Technology Symposium, Senior VP Kevin Zhang said the company has no plans to deploy ASML's next-generation High-NA EUV lithography systems — which cost roughly $400 million each versus about $200 million for current-generation EUV — before 2029, flatly calling the tooling 'too expensive.' Despite a 2026 capex budget of $52–56 billion, TSMC plans to keep using multi-patterning with existing EUV machines and redirect spending toward advanced packaging to reach its 2nm-node goals.
items.whyItMatters: TSMC is ASML's most important customer, so a multi-year delay in High-NA adoption pushes out the ramp of ASML's flagship next-gen product and validates that multi-patterning on existing EUV remains viable through 2029.

items.headline: ServiceNow patches unauthenticated API flaw after attackers queried customer instance tables; advisory updated June 12
items.brief: ServiceNow disclosed a security issue in its `/api/now/related_list_edit/create` endpoint, which was configured with requires_authentication=false and let unauthenticated requests reach sensitive data in hosted customer instances. The company says exploitation activity began June 2, 2026 and that it deployed a fix to hosted instances on June 5; it had received a confidential bug bounty report describing a similar issue on April 22. In an updated advisory, ServiceNow said it believes the observed activity was 'likely tied to security researchers or customer-led research associated with bug bounty submissions rather than malicious threat actors,' and that it is still evaluating whether to publish a CVE.
items.whyItMatters: ServiceNow instances hold IT tickets, employee records and other sensitive enterprise data, so the disclosed access flaw and the ~6-week gap between the bug report and the fix raise customer-trust and disclosure-process questions for a core enterprise vendor.

items.headline: Visa connects its network to OpenAI and rolls out AI-agent and stablecoin tools at Visa Payments Forum 2026
items.brief: At its Payments Forum on June 10–11, Visa unveiled agentic-commerce capabilities — Agent Scoring, an Agentic Registry and a Large Transaction Model — and integrated VisaNet with OpenAI so consumers can have AI agents like ChatGPT pay bills and make purchases. Visa said it has moved billions in stablecoins across VisaNet at an annualized run rate of roughly $7 billion as of March 2026, with 160+ stablecoin-linked card programs live or in development globally.
items.whyItMatters: Positions Visa at the center of AI-driven 'agentic' payments and stablecoin settlement, defending its network as commerce shifts to autonomous agents.

items.headline: CME Group to launch smaller, 24/7 WTI crude and gold contracts for retail traders
items.brief: CME Group said on June 11 it will add round-the-clock trading in smaller-sized energy and metals products, pending regulatory review. A new WTI crude oil contract one-tenth the size of its Micro WTI futures launches August 30, while 24/7 trading on its existing 1-Ounce Gold futures begins July 26.
items.whyItMatters: Extends CME's reach into retail and crypto-style continuous trading, opening a new volume source beyond its institutional base.

items.headline: DBS to offer tokenised physical gold to retail customers via digibank app in H2 2026
items.brief: Singapore's largest bank said on June 11 it will let retail customers digitally buy, hold and trade tokenised physical gold through its digibank app in the second half of 2026 — a first in Singapore for retail clients. Each token represents one gram of gold (about S$200) stored in a DBS Singapore vault and is redeemable for physical metal; DBS said physical gold holdings among its wealth clients have more than doubled over the past three years.
items.whyItMatters: Deepens DBS's digital-asset franchise and supports Singapore's bid to become a gold-trading hub, broadening retail access to a fast-growing asset class.

items.title: FCF Yield (rendimento del flusso di cassa libero)
items.body: Il Free Cash Flow Yield misura quanta cassa libera genera un'azienda rispetto a quanto vale in borsa. La formula in parole: flusso di cassa libero (cassa operativa meno investimenti) diviso la capitalizzazione di mercato (o l'enterprise value). A differenza degli utili contabili, il free cash flow è difficile da truccare: è la cassa vera che resta dopo aver pagato tutto e investito per mantenere il business. Per un investitore retail è un buon controllo della solidità: un rendimento alto e stabile spesso indica un'azienda che 'stampa cassa', uno basso che stai pagando molto per poca generazione reale di denaro.
items.example: Azienda da 10 miliardi di capitalizzazione che genera 500 milioni di FCF → FCF yield = 500/10.000 = 5%. È come dire che il business ti 'rende' il 5% in cassa vera all'anno, prima di crescita.
items.whyItMatters: È la metrica più difficile da manipolare: ti dice se gli utili dichiarati si traducono in soldi veri.

items.title: Liquidation preference
items.body: La liquidation preference è una clausola del term sheet che stabilisce chi viene pagato per primo, e quanto, quando la startup viene venduta o liquidata. Gli investitori con preferenza '1x' recuperano prima il loro capitale investito, e solo dopo si divide il resto con i fondatori. Esistono versioni più aggressive ('participating') in cui l'investitore prima riprende i suoi soldi e poi partecipa anche alla divisione del resto, riducendo molto la quota dei fondatori. Conta perché un'exit che sembra buona può lasciare poco ai fondatori se le preferenze accumulate sono alte.
items.example: Investitore mette 10M con 1x participating. L'azienda viene venduta a 30M: l'investitore prima riprende i suoi 10M, poi partecipa anche alla divisione dei restanti 20M. I fondatori ricevono molto meno del loro 'pezzo nominale'.
items.whyItMatters: Spiega perché i fondatori a volte guadagnano poco da un'exit che ai giornali sembra un successo.

items.title: Quantità di moto (momentum)
items.body: La quantità di moto è 'quanto è difficile fermare qualcosa che si muove': dipende sia dalla massa sia dalla velocità. Un oggetto pesante o veloce ne ha tanta, uno leggero o lento ne ha poca. L'idea centrale è che in un sistema isolato la quantità di moto totale si conserva: non sparisce, si trasferisce da un corpo all'altro. Per questo quando due cose si urtano, il movimento 'passa' dall'una all'altra invece di svanire.
items.example: Un TIR a 50 km/h e una bici a 50 km/h hanno la stessa velocità, ma fermare il TIR è quasi impossibile: ha molta più massa, quindi molta più quantità di moto. Nel biliardo, la palla che colpisce si ferma e 'passa' il suo moto a quella colpita.
items.whyItMatters: Spiega perché conta sia il peso sia la velocità in ogni urto, dagli incidenti agli sport.

items.title: Sfumato di Leonardo da Vinci
items.body: Lo sfumato è la tecnica di sfumare i contorni e i passaggi di luce e ombra in modo così graduale da eliminare ogni linea netta, come fumo che si dissolve nell'aria. Leonardo la portò al massimo: invece di disegnare un bordo del viso, sovrapponeva veli sottilissimi di colore per creare transizioni morbide e impercettibili. Il risultato è un senso di profondità, atmosfera e vita: la pelle sembra respirare e le espressioni diventano ambigue, mutevoli. È il motivo per cui il sorriso della Gioconda sembra cambiare a seconda di dove guardi.
items.example: Negli angoli della bocca e degli occhi della Monna Lisa non c'è nessun contorno preciso: l'ombra si fonde lì, lasciando al cervello il compito di 'completare' l'espressione, che così pare sfuggente.
items.whyItMatters: Capisci perché alcuni volti dipinti sembrano vivi mentre altri sembrano disegnati e piatti.

items.title: Il container e la nascita della globalizzazione
items.body: Negli anni '50 caricare una nave era lentissimo e costosissimo: file di scaricatori spostavano a mano casse di ogni forma, e il trasporto poteva valere fino al 25% del prezzo di un prodotto. Nel 1956 l'imprenditore americano Malcom McLean ebbe l'idea di standardizzare tutto in scatoloni metallici identici, i container, che si caricavano con le gru e passavano direttamente da nave a camion a treno. Il costo di movimentazione crollò di oltre il 90%, rendendo economicamente sensato produrre dall'altra parte del mondo. È la ragione tecnica per cui oggi una scarpa fatta in Asia costa poco anche da noi.
items.example: Prima del container caricare una nave poteva richiedere giorni e decine di uomini; con i container bastano ore e poche gru, e una nave moderna ne trasporta oltre 20.000.
items.whyItMatters: La catena di fornitura globale che diamo per scontata nasce da una scatola di metallo standard, non da un trattato politico.

items.title: Inversione (Charlie Munger)
items.body: Munger ripeteva un consiglio del matematico Jacobi: 'inverti, inverti sempre'. Invece di chiederti solo come ottenere ciò che vuoi, chiediti cosa garantirebbe il fallimento, e poi evita accuratamente quelle cose. È potente perché evitare gli errori stupidi è spesso più facile e più affidabile che cercare mosse geniali. Funziona perché i disastri hanno cause ricorrenti e identificabili: eliminandole, ciò che resta tende ad andare bene da solo.
items.example: Vuoi una carriera di successo? Inverti: cosa la distruggerebbe di sicuro? Inaffidabilità, smettere di imparare, cattive compagnie, vivere oltre le proprie possibilità. Eliminare queste quattro cose ti porta più lontano di mille trucchi di produttività.
items.whyItMatters: Spesso non devi essere brillante: ti basta evitare con metodo gli errori che rovinano tutto.

items.title: Perché la litografia EUV è il collo di bottiglia dei chip
items.body: Per stampare i circuiti di un microchip si proietta un disegno su una fetta di silicio usando la luce, come un proiettore: più corta è la lunghezza d'onda della luce, più piccoli (e fitti) i dettagli che puoi disegnare. I chip più avanzati richiedono luce ultravioletta estrema (EUV), una lunghezza d'onda così difficile da produrre che serve vaporizzare goccioline di stagno con un laser potentissimo, e la luce dev'essere riflessa da specchi perfetti perché nessuna lente la lascia passare. Una sola azienda al mondo, l'olandese ASML, sa costruire queste macchine, che pesano come un aereo e costano oltre 150 milioni a unità. Per questo controllare l'EUV significa controllare chi può fabbricare i chip di ultima generazione.
items.example: Un singolo macchinario EUV di ASML viaggia in più aerei cargo, e senza di esso nessuna fonderia (TSMC, Samsung, Intel) può produrre i chip più piccoli e potenti.
items.whyItMatters: Spiega perché un'unica azienda olandese è diventata il punto di leva geopolitico nella guerra dei chip.

items.title: Allenamento polarizzato 80/20
items.body: L'allenamento polarizzato dice di passare circa l'80% del tempo di allenamento a bassa intensità (zona facile, in cui puoi parlare) e solo il 20% ad alta intensità, evitando la 'zona grigia' di mezzo. Il motivo: il lavoro lento e abbondante costruisce la base aerobica e i mitocondri senza accumulare troppa fatica, mentre le sessioni dure e brevi spingono il VO2max. Il problema tipico, soprattutto in chi fa HYROX, è allenarsi quasi sempre a intensità media-alta: troppo dura per recuperare, troppo facile per progredire davvero. Polarizzare ti fa accumulare più volume utile e arrivare più fresco alle sessioni che contano.
items.example: Così lo applichi: su 5 sedute settimanali, fai 4 a ritmo veramente conversazionale (corsa lenta, sled leggero, zone 1-2) e solo 1-2 davvero intense (intervalli, simulazioni di gara). Se nelle sedute facili sei senza fiato, stai andando troppo forte.
items.whyItMatters: Più volume facile e meno 'zona grigia' migliorano la resistenza riducendo il rischio di sovraccarico.

lesson.title: I quattro casi tedeschi: la mappa di tutto il corso
lesson.markdown:
# I quattro casi tedeschi: la mappa di tutto il corso Benvenuto alla prima lezione di **Grammatica tedesca**. Diamo per acquisiti alfabeto, pronuncia e presente dei verbi: partiamo dal cuore pulsante della lingua, i **quattro casi** (*die vier Fälle*). Tutta la declinazione di aggettivi, articoli e pronomi che vedremo nelle prossime settimane si appoggia su questa griglia. Oggi la fissiamo bene. ## Che cos'è un caso In tedesco la **funzione** di un nome nella frase non si vede dall'ordine delle parole (come in inglese) né solo dalle preposizioni (come in italiano), ma soprattutto dalla **forma dell'articolo e delle terminazioni**. Cambiare caso significa cambiare la "veste" della parola a seconda del ruolo che svolge. I quattro casi sono: 1. **Nominativ** (nominativo) — il **soggetto**, chi compie l'azione. Risponde a *wer/was?* (chi/che cosa?). 2. **Akkusativ** (accusativo) — il **complemento oggetto diretto**. Risponde a *wen/was?* (chi/che cosa?). 3. **Dativ** (dativo) — il **complemento di termine**, l'oggetto indiretto. Risponde a *wem?* (a chi?). 4. **Genitiv** (genitivo) — il **complemento di specificazione**, il possesso. Risponde a *wessen?* (di chi?). > **Frase-chiave da memorizzare:** *Der Mann (Nom.) gibt dem Kind (Dat.) den Ball (Akk.).* → "L'uomo dà la palla al bambino." Un solo verbo, tre casi diversi, tre vesti diverse. ## La tabella madre: l'articolo determinativo Questa è la tabella più importante del corso. Imparala come una poesia: la useremo in ogni lezione successiva. | Caso | maschile | femminile | neutro | plurale | |------|----------|-----------|--------|---------| | **Nominativ** | **der** Mann | **die** Frau | **das** Kind | **die** Kinder | | **Akkusativ** | **den** Mann | **die** Frau | **das** Kind | **die** Kinder | | **Dativ** | **dem** Mann | **der** Frau | **dem** Kind | **den** Kinder**n** | | **Genitiv** | **des** Mann**es** | **der** Frau | **des** Kind**es** | **der** Kinder | Osserva tre cose decisive: - Cambia **solo il maschile** tra Nominativo e Accusativo (*der → den*). Femminile e neutro restano identici: è la fonte di metà delle paure inutili. - Al **Dativo plurale** il nome aggiunge una **-n** (se non ce l'ha già): *den Kindern*, *den Männern*. - Al **Genitivo** maschile e neutro il nome prende **-(e)s**: *des Mannes*, *des Autos*. L'articolo indeterminativo (*ein/eine*) e i possessivi (*mein, dein…*) seguono **le stesse terminazioni**, tranne tre caselle "scoperte" al Nominativo/Accusativo singolare — le vedremo nella lezione sulla declinazione mista. ## Come scegliere il caso: il diagramma decisionale ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Qual è il ruolo del nome?] --> B{Compie l'azione?} B -->|Sì| N[NOMINATIV
wer/was?] B -->|No| C{C'è una preposizione
che lo regge?} C -->|Sì| P[Il caso lo decide
la preposizione] C -->|No| D{Riceve l'azione
direttamente?} D -->|Sì, oggetto diretto| AK[AKKUSATIV
wen/was?] D -->|È il destinatario| DA[DATIV
wem?] D -->|Esprime possesso| G[GENITIV
wessen?] ``` Il ramo **"c'è una preposizione"** è enorme e sarà il tema di diverse lezioni: alcune preposizioni reggono sempre l'Accusativo (*für, ohne, gegen, durch, um*), altre sempre il Dativo (*mit, nach, aus, bei, seit, von, zu*), altre ancora — le *Wechselpräpositionen* — oscillano tra i due. Per oggi basta sapere che esiste questo "interruttore". ## Esempi realistici - **Nom.** *Der Zug ist pünktlich.* → "Il treno è puntuale." (soggetto) - **Akk.** *Ich nehme den Zug.* → "Prendo il treno." (oggetto diretto) - **Dat.** *Ich danke dem Fahrer.* → "Ringrazio l'autista." (il verbo *danken* regge il Dativo!) - **Gen.** *Das ist der Fahrplan des Zuges.* → "Questo è l'orario del treno." (specificazione) ## Errori tipici da evitare - **Tradurre "a" sempre con il Dativo.** *Ich gehe in die Stadt* ("Vado in città") usa l'Accusativo, perché c'è movimento verso un luogo: il caso lo decide la preposizione, non la preposizione italiana. - **Dimenticare la -n del Dativo plurale.** Non *mit die Kinder*, ma ***mit den Kindern***. - **Usare l'Accusativo dopo verbi che vogliono il Dativo.** *helfen, danken, gefallen, gehören, antworten* reggono il **Dativo**: *Ich helfe dir* (non *dich*). - **Confondere soggetto e oggetto perché l'ordine cambia.** *Den Ball sieht der Hund* significa comunque "Il cane vede la palla": è *der Hund* il soggetto, perché è al Nominativo, non perché viene prima. ## Esercizi **1. Identifica il caso** del nome in grassetto: a) *Wir besuchen **die Großmutter**.* b) *Das Auto **des Lehrers** ist neu.* c) *Ich gebe **dem Hund** Wasser.* **2. Inserisci l'articolo determinativo corretto:** a) *Ich sehe ___ Mann.* (m.) b) *Er hilft ___ Frau.* (f.) c) *Das ist das Ende ___ Films.* (m.) d) *Sie spielt mit ___ Kindern.* (pl.) **3. Correggi l'errore:** a) *Ich danke den Lehrer.* b) *Wir fahren mit die Bus.* **4. Traduci in tedesco:** "L'uomo dà al bambino il libro." --- ### Soluzioni **1.** a) Akkusativ (oggetto diretto) — b) Genitiv (specificazione) — c) Dativ (destinatario). **2.** a) *den* — b) *der* — c) *des* — d) *den*. **3.** a) *Ich danke **dem** Lehrer* (*danken* → Dativo) — b) *Wir fahren mit **dem** Bus* (*mit* → Dativo). **4.** *Der Mann gibt dem Kind das Buch.* (Nom. + Dat. + Akk.) ## Takeaways - I **quattro casi** segnalano la funzione del nome tramite la forma di **articolo e terminazioni**, non tramite l'ordine delle parole. - Memorizza la **tabella dell'articolo determinativo**: è la base di tutta la declinazione del corso. - Solo il **maschile** distingue Nom./Akk.; il **Dativo plurale** aggiunge **-n**; il **Genitivo** m./n. aggiunge **-(e)s**. - Il caso può dipendere dal **verbo** (*helfen, danken* → Dativo) o da una **preposizione** — l'interruttore che esploreremo presto.

lesson.title: La email professionale tedesca: Anrede, struttura e Grußformel
lesson.markdown:
# La email professionale tedesca: Anrede, struttura e Grußformel

Benvenuto al primo passo del nostro percorso di **tedesco professionale**. Partiamo dallo strumento che userai ogni singolo giorno in un ufficio tedesco: la **E-Mail**. In Germania la comunicazione scritta resta sorprendentemente formale e codificata — sbagliare la formula d'apertura o di chiusura comunica subito *incompetenza culturale*. Oggi costruiamo lo scheletro completo di una email corretta.

## L'architettura di una email

Ogni email professionale ha cinque blocchi fissi. Vederli come uno schema ti aiuta a non dimenticarne nessuno.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Betreff — Oggetto] --> B[Anrede — Formula di apertura]
    B --> C[Einleitung — Frase introduttiva]
    C --> D[Hauptteil — Corpo del messaggio]
    D --> E[Schlusssatz — Frase di chiusura]
    E --> F[Grußformel — Saluto finale + nome]
```

### 1. Der Betreff (l'oggetto)

Breve, concreto, senza articolo se possibile. Esempi:
- **Anfrage: Angebot für 50 Lizenzen** → *Richiesta: offerta per 50 licenze*
- **Terminbestätigung: Meeting am 18. Juni** → *Conferma appuntamento: riunione del 18 giugno*

### 2. Die Anrede (la formula di apertura)

Qui si decide il **registro**. La regola d'oro: **Sie** (Lei formale) è il default assoluto nel mondo del lavoro tedesco. Non passare mai al **du** (tu) per primo se sei il junior o l'esterno — aspetti che te lo propongano (das **Du anbieten**).

| Situazione | Anrede | Traduzione |
|---|---|---|
| Persona nota, nome noto | **Sehr geehrter Herr Müller,** | Egregio Sig. Müller, |
| Persona nota (donna) | **Sehr geehrte Frau Schmidt,** | Gentile Sig.ra Schmidt, |
| Più destinatari | **Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren,** | Egregi Signore e Signori, |
| Contatto già consolidato | **Liebe Frau Schmidt,** | Cara Sig.ra Schmidt, |
| Collega con cui si dà del *du* | **Hallo Anna,** / **Hallo zusammen,** | Ciao Anna / Ciao a tutti, |

> ⚠️ Attenzione a due dettagli che gli italiani sbagliano sempre:
> 1. Dopo la **Anrede** si mette **una virgola**, e la riga successiva inizia in **minuscola** (a meno che non sia un sostantivo, sempre maiuscolo in tedesco).
> 2. **Sehr geehrte*r*** concorda col genere: *-r* per Herr (maschile), niente *-r* per Frau (femminile).

### 3. Die Einleitung (la frase introduttiva)

Il tedesco va dritto al motivo. Frasi-modello (*Redemittel*) pronte all'uso:

- **Ich schreibe Ihnen bezüglich …** → *Le scrivo in merito a …*
- **vielen Dank für Ihre E-Mail vom 12. Juni.** → *grazie per la Sua email del 12 giugno.*
- **wie telefonisch besprochen, sende ich Ihnen …** → *come concordato telefonicamente, Le invio …*
- **ich beziehe mich auf Ihr Angebot vom …** → *faccio riferimento alla Vostra offerta del …*

### 4. Der Hauptteil (il corpo)

Una frase = un'idea. Usa connettori chiari: **außerdem** (*inoltre*), **allerdings** (*tuttavia*), **daher** (*pertanto*). Per le richieste, il **Konjunktiv II** rende tutto più cortese:

- **Könnten Sie mir bitte …?** → *Potrebbe per favore …?*
- **Ich würde mich freuen, wenn …** → *Mi farebbe piacere se …*

### 5. Schlusssatz + Grußformel (chiusura e saluto)

Frase di chiusura tipica:
- **Für Rückfragen stehe ich Ihnen gerne zur Verfügung.** → *Resto a disposizione per eventuali domande.*
- **Ich freue mich auf Ihre Rückmeldung.** → *Attendo un Suo riscontro.*

Poi la **Grußformel**, da scegliere in base al registro:

| Registro | Grußformel | Traduzione |
|---|---|---|
| Standard formale | **Mit freundlichen Grüßen** | Cordiali saluti |
| Molto formale | **Mit freundlichen Grüßen aus Berlin** | (cordiali saluti, + luogo) |
| Semi-formale | **Beste Grüße** / **Viele Grüße** | Cari saluti |
| Informale (colleghi) | **Liebe Grüße** / **LG** | Saluti affettuosi |

> Dopo la **Grußformel** **non** si mette la virgola. Va a capo, e segue il tuo nome.

## Testo-modello completo

> **Betreff:** Anfrage: Angebot für Projektmanagement-Software
>
> **Sehr geehrte Frau Becker,**
>
> ich schreibe Ihnen bezüglich Ihrer Software für Projektmanagement. Unser Team sucht derzeit eine neue Lösung für etwa 30 Mitarbeiter.
>
> **Könnten Sie** mir bitte ein **Angebot** für 30 Lizenzen zusenden? Außerdem **würde ich mich freuen**, wenn Sie mir mitteilen könnten, ob eine kostenlose Testphase möglich ist.
>
> Für Rückfragen stehe ich Ihnen gerne zur Verfügung. Ich freue mich auf Ihre **Rückmeldung**.
>
> **Mit freundlichen Grüßen**
> Andrea Rossi
> Einkauf, Rossi GmbH

*Traduzione del corpo:* «Le scrivo in merito al Vostro software per la gestione progetti. Il nostro team cerca attualmente una nuova soluzione per circa 30 collaboratori. Potrebbe inviarmi un'offerta per 30 licenze? Inoltre mi farebbe piacere se potesse comunicarmi se è possibile un periodo di prova gratuito. Resto a disposizione per eventuali domande. Attendo un Suo riscontro.»

## Note d'uso e registro

- **Mai usare "Hallo" o "Hi" con un cliente o un superiore esterno** alla prima email: suona troppo casual.
- La forma **Sie** richiede sempre la maiuscola (**Sie, Ihnen, Ihr**) — è una cortesia ortografica, non un errore.
- Il tedesco preferisce la **voce attiva e concisa** a giri di parole. Una email troppo lunga è percepita come poco professionale.
- **z. B.** (= *zum Beispiel*, «per esempio») e **u. a.** (= *unter anderem*, «tra l'altro») sono abbreviazioni standard accettate.

## Takeaways

- Una email tedesca ha **5 blocchi fissi**: Betreff, Anrede, Einleitung, Hauptteil, Grußformel.
- Il default è sempre il **Sie** formale; aspetta che ti venga offerto il **du**.
- **Anrede → virgola → riga successiva in minuscola**; **Grußformel → nessuna virgola**.
- **Sehr geehrte*r* Herr / Sehr geehrte Frau** è la formula formale standard, **Mit freundlichen Grüßen** la chiusura standard.
- Usa il **Konjunktiv II** (*könnten Sie, ich würde mich freuen*) per richieste cortesi.

lesson.title: Fundamentals: Latency, Throughput, and How Systems Scale
lesson.markdown:
# Fundamentals: Latency, Throughput, and How Systems Scale Welcome to **System Design**. Over this course we'll build, piece by piece, the toolkit for designing systems that serve millions of users without falling over: load balancers, caches, sharded databases, message queues, CDNs, and more. But every one of those tools exists to push against the same two walls. So today we start at the foundation — the vocabulary and the trade-offs that everything else will rest on. By the end of this lesson you should be able to say precisely what it means for a system to be "fast," what it means for it to "handle load," and why those are *not the same thing*. ## Latency vs. Throughput These are the two numbers that define performance, and confusing them is the most common beginner mistake. - **Latency** is the time to handle a *single* request — how long *one* user waits. Measured in milliseconds (ms). - **Throughput** is the number of requests handled *per unit of time* — the system's capacity. Measured in requests per second (RPS) or queries per second (QPS). A useful analogy is a highway. **Latency** is how long it takes *one car* to drive from A to B. **Throughput** is how many cars pass a given point *per minute*. Widening the highway (more lanes) raises throughput without making any single car faster. Raising the speed limit lowers latency for everyone. > Crucially, you can improve one while the other gets *worse*. Batching requests together often raises throughput (fewer per-request overheads) but raises latency (each request waits for the batch to fill). ### Don't trust the average When we report latency, the **average** lies. If 99 requests take 10 ms and one takes 5,000 ms, the average is ~60 ms — but real users *felt* that 5-second stall. We therefore report **percentiles**: - $p50$ (median): half of requests are faster than this. - $p99$ ("tail latency"): 99% are faster; the worst 1% are slower. A blunt way to state the relationship between throughput and latency under load is **Little's Law**: $$L = \lambda \cdot W$$ where $L$ is the average number of requests *in the system* concurrently, $\lambda$ is the arrival rate (throughput), and $W$ is the average time each request spends in the system (latency). It tells us that if requests arrive faster than we can finish them, work *piles up* — and queued work means rising latency. This is why a system near its throughput ceiling feels sluggish long before it fully breaks. ## The two ways to scale When one machine can no longer keep up, you have exactly two moves. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Demand exceeds capacity] --> B{How to add capacity?} B -->|Vertical| C["Scale UP
bigger machine:
more CPU, RAM, faster disk"] B -->|Horizontal| D["Scale OUT
more machines
working together"] C --> E[Simple, but a hard ceiling
and a single point of failure] D --> F[No ceiling, fault-tolerant
but adds coordination complexity] ``` ### Vertical scaling (scale up) Buy a **bigger box**: more CPU cores, more RAM, faster SSDs. - **Pros:** Dead simple. Your code doesn't change. No distributed-systems headaches. - **Cons:** There is a *physical ceiling* — you can't buy an infinitely large server, and the price grows faster than the power. Worse, it's a **single point of failure (SPOF)**: when that one machine dies, your whole service dies. ### Horizontal scaling (scale out) Add **more machines** and spread the work across them. - **Pros:** Effectively *unbounded* — need more capacity, add more nodes. And it's **fault-tolerant**: if one node dies, the others carry on. - **Cons:** Now you have a *distributed system*. You need something to spread requests (a **load balancer**, our next lesson), and you must confront state: where does a user's data live? How do nodes stay consistent? Most of this course exists to answer those questions. | | Vertical (up) | Horizontal (out) | |---|---|---| | How | Bigger machine | More machines | | Ceiling | Hardware limit | Practically none | | Failure | Single point of failure | Tolerant by design | | Complexity | Low | High (coordination) | | Cost curve | Superlinear | Roughly linear | The defining feature that *enables* horizontal scaling is **statelessness**. If each application server keeps no per-user state in its own memory — pushing session data to a shared store instead — then any server can handle any request, and you can add or remove servers freely. Stateful nodes can scale out too, but only with the replication and sharding machinery we'll cover later. ## A mental model for the whole course Almost every technique ahead is a specific answer to *"we hit a wall — latency too high, or throughput too low."* - **Caching** → cut latency by not redoing work. - **Load balancing** → raise throughput by spreading work across many nodes. - **Replication / sharding** → scale the *database* horizontally. - **Queues** → absorb traffic spikes so throughput stays steady under bursts. Keep asking of every design: *which wall does this push against, and what does it cost me?* There is no free lunch — only trade-offs you choose deliberately. ## Takeaways - **Latency** = how long *one* request takes; **throughput** = how many requests *per second*. Optimizing one can hurt the other. - Report **percentiles** ($p50$, $p99$), never just the average — tail latency is what users feel. - **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$) explains why a system near capacity slows to a crawl: work queues up. - **Vertical scaling** (bigger box) is simple but capped and a single point of failure; **horizontal scaling** (more boxes) is unbounded and fault-tolerant but adds coordination complexity. - **Statelessness** is what makes horizontal scaling easy — keep per-user state out of individual servers. - Every tool in this course pushes against the latency wall or the throughput wall, at some cost. Always name the trade-off.

lesson.title: What Is a Language Model? Tokens and the Probability of Text
lesson.markdown:
# What Is a Language Model? Tokens and the Probability of Text Welcome to the first lesson of **LLM & AI Engineering**. Before we touch embeddings, attention, or agents, we need to be precise about the thing at the center of it all. Strip away the chat interfaces and the hype, and a large language model is something surprisingly concrete: *a function that assigns probabilities to sequences of tokens*. Today we'll make that sentence exact, and we'll meet **tokens** — the atoms every model actually reads and writes. ## A language model is a probability distribution over text A **language model (LM)** is a model that, given some text so far, predicts what comes next. Formally, it defines a probability distribution over sequences of tokens. Using the chain rule of probability, the probability of a whole sequence $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T$ factorizes into a product of *next-token* predictions: $$P(x_1, \dots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_1, \dots, x_{t-1})$$ Here each factor $P(x_t \mid x_{ **Key idea:** An LLM never "knows" an answer in one shot. It repeatedly answers one narrow question — *"given this context, what token is likely next?"* — and the fluent paragraphs you see are thousands of those tiny predictions chained together. That's it. The intelligence, the apparent reasoning, the style — all of it emerges from a model that got extraordinarily good at one humble task: completing text. ## Tokens: the real units of language for an LLM Models do not see words, and they certainly don't see raw characters one by one in any efficient way. They see **tokens** — chunks of text drawn from a fixed **vocabulary** (typically 30,000–200,000 entries). A token might be a whole word, a word fragment, a single character, punctuation, or even a space. Modern LLMs use **subword tokenization**, most commonly a scheme called **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**. The intuition: - Common words get their own single token: `" the"`, `" model"`. - Rare or novel words get split into pieces: `"tokenization"` → `"token"` + `"ization"`. - This lets a finite vocabulary represent *any* string, including names, typos, and code, while keeping frequent text compact. A rough rule of thumb for English: **1 token ≈ 4 characters ≈ 0.75 words**. So ~1,000 tokens is roughly 750 words. This matters directly for cost and context limits, which we'll study later. Here is the journey from your text to a prediction and back: ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raw text:
'The cat sat'"] --> B["Tokenizer
(BPE)"] B --> C["Token IDs:
[464, 3797, 3332]"] C --> D["Language Model"] D --> E["Distribution over
next token"] E --> F["Sample / pick
token → ' on'"] F --> G["Append, repeat"] G --> D ``` Notice the loop at the end: generation is iterative. Each token the model emits is fed back in as part of the context for the next step. ### Why tokens, not words or characters? | Unit | Vocabulary size | Sequence length | Problem | |---|---|---|---| | **Characters** | Tiny (~100) | Very long | Sequences become huge; model must learn spelling from scratch | | **Words** | Huge (millions) | Short | Can't handle unseen words; vocabulary explodes | | **Subwords (BPE)** | Moderate (~50k–200k) | Moderate | *The practical sweet spot* | Subword tokenization is the engineering compromise that makes everything else feasible. ## A concrete walk-through Suppose the context is **"The capital of France is"**. The tokenizer turns this into a handful of token IDs. The model processes them and outputs a probability for *every* token in its vocabulary as the next one. It might look like: ``` P(" Paris") = 0.91 P(" the") = 0.02 P(" located")= 0.01 P(" a") = 0.01 ... (tens of thousands more, each tiny) ``` The model then **samples** from this distribution (we'll cover *how* — temperature, top-p — in the inference lesson). It appends the chosen token and repeats. Output the token `" Paris"`, feed `"The capital of France is Paris"` back in, and ask again. > **A consequence worth remembering:** because the model picks one token at a time without a global plan, behaviors like *hallucination* and *getting stuck in repetition* aren't bugs bolted on — they're inherent to a system that locally optimizes "plausible next token." Engineering around this is much of what AI engineering *is*. ## Why "large"? The "large" in LLM refers to two things growing together: 1. **Parameters** — the billions of learned numbers (weights) inside the model. More parameters mean more capacity to store patterns. 2. **Training data** — trillions of tokens of text scraped from books, code, and the web. When you scale both, models cross thresholds where qualitatively new abilities appear — translation, arithmetic, code generation — without being explicitly programmed for them. These **emergent abilities** are why the field exploded. But never lose sight of the foundation: underneath the scale, the objective is still just *next-token prediction*. ## How this sets up the rest of the course Everything ahead builds on today: - **Embeddings** (next lesson): how a token ID like `3797` becomes a vector of numbers the model can compute with. - **The transformer & attention**: the architecture that computes $P(x_t \mid x_{
